course: 21-254 Vector Calculus and Linear Algebra for Engineers
contentPolicyNote: Original SI-style problems inspired by the uploaded course calendar, notes, and practice exams. Do not copy official homework or exam wording. Solutions are independently worked out and should be reviewed before publishing.
studentFacingDescription: Practice standard matrices for linear transformations, one-to-one and onto reasoning, determinant computations and properties, inverse matrices, and the invertibility connections that show up around the end of Exam 1 and start of Exam 2.

recommendedFlow: Read the problem and try it on paper first.
Reveal hints one at a time.
Reveal the setup before the full solution.
Reveal the full worked solution and common mistakes.

problems.title: Standard Matrix, Invertibility, and Solving \(T(x)=b\)
skills: Write the standard matrix of a transformation
problems.problemLatex:
Define \(T:\mathbb{R}^3\to\mathbb{R}^3\) by
\[T(x,y,z)=\begin{bmatrix}x-2y+z\\3x+y\\y-4z\end{bmatrix}.\]

(a) Find the standard matrix \(A\) for \(T\).

(b) Determine whether \(T\) is one-to-one and onto.

(c) Find the unique vector \(v\) such that \(T(v)=\begin{bmatrix}1\\2\\-3\end{bmatrix}\), if it exists.
problems.thinkFirst: A transformation written by coordinate formulas is already telling you the columns of its standard matrix. The determinant will decide invertibility for a square matrix.
hints: The first coordinate is \(x-2y+z\), so the first row of the standard matrix is \([1,-2,1]\).
For a \(3	imes 3\) matrix, nonzero determinant means one-to-one and onto.
Solve \(Av=b\) after you know the matrix is invertible.
problems.setupLatex:
The standard matrix is
\[A=\begin{bmatrix}1&-2&1\\3&1&0\\0&1&-4\end{bmatrix}.\]
Compute \(\det(A)\) and then solve \(Av=b\).

solutionSteps.label: Decide one-to-one and onto
solutionSteps.latex: Because \(A\) is invertible, \(T\) is both one-to-one and onto as a map from \(\mathbb{R}^3\) to \(\mathbb{R}^3\).

problems.finalAnswerLatex:
\[A=\begin{bmatrix}1&-2&1\\3&1&0\\0&1&-4\end{bmatrix},\qquad \det(A)=-25.\]
Thus \(T\) is one-to-one and onto, and
\[v=\begin{bmatrix}3/5\\1/5\\4/5\end{bmatrix}.\]
commonMistakes: Putting the coefficient rows in as columns incorrectly.
Saying one-to-one but not onto even though the matrix is square and invertible.
Solving \(A^Tv=b\) instead of \(Av=b\).
problems.similarProblemPrompt: Change one coefficient so that the determinant becomes zero, then decide how the one-to-one/onto answer changes.

problems.title: Find a Transformation from Its Values on Two Vectors
skills: Find a standard matrix from nonstandard input vectors
problems.problemLatex:
Let \(T:\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}^2\) be linear. Suppose
\[T\!\left(\begin{bmatrix}1\\1\end{bmatrix}\right)=\begin{bmatrix}4\\1\end{bmatrix},\qquad
T\!\left(\begin{bmatrix}2\\-1\end{bmatrix}\right)=\begin{bmatrix}1\\5\end{bmatrix}.\]

(a) Find the standard matrix \(A\) of \(T\).

(b) Compute \(T\!\left(\begin{bmatrix}3\\2\end{bmatrix}\right)\).
problems.thinkFirst: The given input vectors are not \(e_1\) and \(e_2\), so the output vectors are not directly the columns of \(A\). Put the input vectors into a matrix \(P\) and use \(AP=Y\).
hints: Let \(P\) have the given input vectors as columns.
Let \(Y\) have the corresponding output vectors as columns.
Then \(AP=Y\), so \(A=YP^{-1}\).
problems.setupLatex:
Set
\[P=\begin{bmatrix}1&2\\1&-1\end{bmatrix},\qquad Y=\begin{bmatrix}4&1\\1&5\end{bmatrix}.\]
Since the columns of \(P\) are independent, \(P\) is invertible.
commonMistakes: Treating the given output vectors as the columns of \(A\).
Forgetting to invert the input matrix \(P\).
Putting the input vectors as rows instead of columns.
problems.similarProblemPrompt: Try rewriting \([3,2]^T\) as a linear combination of the two given input vectors, then use linearity instead of finding \(A\).

problems.title: Determinant by Row Operations
skills: Use row replacement without changing determinant
problems.problemLatex:
Compute the determinant of
\[A=\begin{bmatrix}2&1&0\\4&3&1\\-2&1&5\end{bmatrix}.\]
Then decide whether \(A\) is invertible.
problems.thinkFirst: Use row replacement operations to create zeros. Row replacement does not change the determinant, but row swaps and row scaling do.
hints: You can expand along a row or use row operations.
Try \(R_2\leftarrow R_2-2R_1\) and \(R_3\leftarrow R_3+R_1\).
A square matrix is invertible exactly when its determinant is nonzero.
commonMistakes: Changing the determinant after a row replacement.
Forgetting that row scaling changes determinant.
Stopping at triangular form but forgetting to multiply diagonal entries.
problems.similarProblemPrompt: Compute the determinant again by cofactor expansion along the first row and compare.

problems.title: Inverse Matrix and a Linear System
problems.problemLatex:
Let
\[A=\begin{bmatrix}1&2&0\\0&1&3\\2&0&1\end{bmatrix},\qquad b=\begin{bmatrix}5\\7\\4\end{bmatrix}.\]

(a) Find \(\det(A)\).

(b) Find \(A^{-1}\).

(c) Use your inverse to solve \(Ax=b\).
problems.thinkFirst: The determinant tells you whether the inverse exists. Once you have \(A^{-1}\), the solution is \(x=A^{-1}b\).
hints: If row reducing \([A\mid I]\), keep track of all three columns of the identity matrix.
You should get determinant \(13\), so fractions with denominator 13 are expected.
Multiply \(A^{-1}b\) carefully.
problems.setupLatex: The inverse exists because \(\det(A)=13\). Row reducing \([A\mid I]\) or using cofactors gives the same inverse.
commonMistakes: Trying to divide by \(A\) instead of multiplying by \(A^{-1}\).
Forgetting that every entry of the inverse is divided by 13.
Making arithmetic errors while multiplying \(A^{-1}b\).
problems.similarProblemPrompt: Solve the same system by row reducing \([A\mid b]\), then compare the result.

problems.title: A Parameter That Controls Invertibility
skills: Compute a determinant with a parameter
Find values that make a matrix singular
problems.problemLatex:
For
\[A_k=\begin{bmatrix}1&2&k\\0&1&3\\2&0&1\end{bmatrix},\]
find all values of \(k\) for which \(A_k\) is invertible.
problems.thinkFirst: For a square matrix, invertibility is equivalent to nonzero determinant. Compute \(\det(A_k)\) as an expression in \(k\).
hints: Expand along the second row or first column if it feels easier.
The determinant should be linear in \(k\).
Invertible means determinant not equal to zero.

solutionSteps.label: Set the nonzero condition
solutionSteps.latex:
The matrix is invertible exactly when
\[13-2k\neq 0.\]

solutionSteps.label: Solve for \(k\)
solutionSteps.latex:
\[13-2k=0\quad\Rightarrow\quad k=\frac{13}{2}.\]
Therefore \(A_k\) is invertible for every \(k\neq 13/2\).

commonMistakes: Solving \(\det(A_k)=0\) and reporting that as the invertible set.
Dropping the factor of 2 in \(13-2k\).
Assuming any parameter matrix is automatically invertible unless a row is visibly zero.
problems.similarProblemPrompt: At \(k=13/2\), row reduce the matrix to find a nonzero vector in its null space.

problems.title: Area Scaling Under a Linear Map
skills: Use determinant as an area scale factor
Compute the sign and magnitude of a determinant
Apply a linear map to geometry
problems.problemLatex:
Let \(T:\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}^2\) have standard matrix
\[A=\begin{bmatrix}2&-1\\3&4\end{bmatrix}.\]

(a) What factor does \(T\) multiply areas by?

(b) Does \(T\) preserve or reverse orientation?

(c) If a region \(R\) has area \(7\), what is the area of \(T(R)\)?
problems.thinkFirst: For a \(2	imes 2\) linear map, \(|\det(A)|\) is the area scale factor, while the sign of \(\det(A)\) tracks orientation.
hints: Compute \(ad-bc\).
Use absolute value for area.
Positive determinant preserves orientation.

solutionSteps.label: Interpret area scaling
solutionSteps.latex: The area scale factor is \(|\det(A)|=11\). So every area is multiplied by 11.

problems.finalAnswerLatex: The area scale factor is \(11\), orientation is preserved, and \(\operatorname{area}(T(R))=77\).
commonMistakes: Using \(\det(A)\) without absolute value for area.
Thinking positive determinant means lengths are preserved.
Using trace instead of determinant.
problems.similarProblemPrompt: Try a matrix with negative determinant and explain what changes geometrically.

problems.title: Kernel and Image of a Linear Transformation
skills: Find the matrix of a transformation
Decide one-to-one and onto for non-square transformations
problems.problemLatex:
Define \(T:\mathbb{R}^3\to\mathbb{R}^2\) by
\[T(x,y,z)=\begin{bmatrix}x+y\\y+z\end{bmatrix}.\]

(a) Find the standard matrix \(A\) of \(T\).

(b) Find a basis for \(\ker(T)\).

(c) Is \(T\) one-to-one? Is \(T\) onto \(\mathbb{R}^2\)? Explain.
problems.thinkFirst: The kernel is the solution set to \(T(x,y,z)=0\). The image is the column space of the standard matrix.
hints: Write the matrix first.
Solve \(x+y=0\) and \(y+z=0\).
A map from \(\mathbb{R}^3\) to \(\mathbb{R}^2\) cannot be one-to-one if its kernel is nontrivial.
problems.setupLatex:
The standard matrix is
\[A=\begin{bmatrix}1&1&0\\0&1&1\end{bmatrix}.\]
Now solve \(A\begin{bmatrix}x\\y\\z\end{bmatrix}=0\).

solutionSteps.label: Solve for the kernel
solutionSteps.latex:
The kernel satisfies
\[x+y=0,\qquad y+z=0.\]
Let \(y=t\). Then \(x=-t\) and \(z=-t\), so
\[\begin{bmatrix}x\\y\\z\end{bmatrix}=t\begin{bmatrix}-1\\1\\-1\end{bmatrix}.\]

solutionSteps.label: Decide one-to-one
solutionSteps.latex: Since \(\ker(T)=\operatorname{span}\{[-1,1,-1]^T\}\) is nontrivial, \(T\) is not one-to-one.

solutionSteps.label: Decide onto
solutionSteps.latex: The columns \(\begin{bmatrix}1\\0\end{bmatrix}\) and \(\begin{bmatrix}1\\1\end{bmatrix}\) are linearly independent in \(\mathbb{R}^2\), so the column space is all of \(\mathbb{R}^2\). Thus \(T\) is onto \(\mathbb{R}^2\).

problems.finalAnswerLatex:
\[A=\begin{bmatrix}1&1&0\\0&1&1\end{bmatrix},\qquad \ker(T)=\operatorname{span}\left\{\begin{bmatrix}-1\\1\\-1\end{bmatrix}\right\}.\]
The transformation is not one-to-one, but it is onto \(\mathbb{R}^2\).
commonMistakes: Forgetting that the kernel lives in the domain \(\mathbb{R}^3\).
Saying not onto just because there are three input variables.
Confusing columns of the matrix with rows when finding the image.
problems.similarProblemPrompt: Modify the second component to \(2x+2y\) and decide whether the new map is onto.

problems.title: Determinant Properties Without Expanding
problems.problemLatex:
Let \(A\) and \(B\) be \(3\times 3\) matrices with
\[\det(A)=-2,
\qquad \det(B)=5.\]

Compute:

(a) \(\det(A^T)\)

(b) \(\det(B^{-1})\)

(c) \(\det(3A)\)

(d) \(\det(A^TB^{-1}A^2)\)
problems.thinkFirst: Use determinant rules instead of trying to invent matrices. Remember that multiplying a \(3	imes 3\) matrix by 3 scales the determinant by \(3^3\), not by 3.
hints: \(\det(A^T)=\det(A)\).
\(\det(cA)=c^n\det(A)\) for \(n	imes n\) matrices.
problems.setupLatex: Apply each determinant identity separately, then multiply the determinant factors in part (d).
commonMistakes: Using \(\det(3A)=3\det(A)\) instead of \(3^3\det(A)\).
Forgetting that \(\det(A^2)=\det(A)^2\).
Changing the determinant when transposing.
problems.similarProblemPrompt: Repeat part (c) for a \(2	imes 2\) matrix and compare the scalar exponent.

problems.title: Invertibility Theorem Checkpoint
skills: Connect pivots, determinant, null space, and columns
problems.problemLatex:
Let \(A\) be a \(4\times 4\) matrix. Decide whether each statement is true or false. Give a brief explanation.

(a) If \(A\) has a pivot in every row, then \(A\) is invertible.

(b) If \(Ax=0\) has only the trivial solution, then \(\det(A)\neq 0\).

(c) If the columns of \(A\) span \(\mathbb{R}^4\), then the columns of \(A\) are linearly independent.

(d) If \(A\) is not invertible, then \(Ax=b\) has no solution for every \(b\in\mathbb{R}^4\).
problems.thinkFirst: For square matrices, many conditions are equivalent: full pivots, independent columns, spanning columns, trivial null space, nonzero determinant, and invertibility. Part (d) is the tricky one.
hints: In a square matrix, a pivot in every row also means a pivot in every column.
Trivial null space is equivalent to invertibility for square matrices.
For \(4	imes 4\), spanning four-dimensional space forces four independent columns.
Not invertible means at least one \(b\) fails, not every \(b\).
problems.setupLatex: Use the invertible matrix theorem, but read each quantifier carefully.

solutionSteps.label: Part (a)
solutionSteps.latex: True. Since \(A\) is \(4\times 4\), a pivot in every row means there are 4 pivots, so there is also a pivot in every column. Therefore \(A\) is invertible.

solutionSteps.label: Part (b)
solutionSteps.latex: True. If \(Ax=0\) has only the trivial solution, then the columns are linearly independent, so \(A\) is invertible and \(\det(A)\neq 0\).

solutionSteps.label: Part (c)
solutionSteps.latex: True. Four columns that span \(\mathbb{R}^4\) must have rank 4, so all four columns are pivot columns and are linearly independent.

solutionSteps.label: Part (d)
solutionSteps.latex: False. If \(A\) is not invertible, then \(Ax=b\) is not consistent for every \(b\), but some choices of \(b\) may still work. For example, \(b=0\) always gives at least the solution \(x=0\).

commonMistakes: Forgetting the matrix is square.
Confusing “not every \(b\)” with “no \(b\).”
Thinking \(Ax=0\) can ever be inconsistent.
problems.similarProblemPrompt: Write the same true/false list for a \(3	imes 5\) matrix and notice which equivalences break.
